name: Badarayana
dates_notes: Date range spans four centuries; identity with Vyāsa is traditional, not academic; only the Brahma-Sūtra survives, and even its layers may be multi-author.
core_thesis: What discipline does a Vedāntic inquirer enter, and on what authority? Bādarāyaṇa answers in his first two *sūtras*. *Athāto brahma-jijñāsā* (1.1.1) — "now, therefore, the inquiry into *brahman*" — opens the discipline, and *janmādy asya yataḥ* (1.1.2) defines its object as "that from which the origin and the rest of this [world]" proceeds. *Brahman* is thus introduced under one descriptive function: the *jagat-kāraṇa*, world-cause. The work then unfolds across four *adhyāyas* whose headings name four argumentative moves: *samanvaya* (harmonization of *śruti* texts as teaching *brahman*), *avirodha* (rebuttal of rival cosmologies), *sādhana* (the means: meditative *upāsanā*, Vedic *karma*'s relation to knowledge), *phala* (the fruit: *mokṣa* and the trajectory of the *jīva* after death). On the *jīva*–*brahman* relation Bādarāyaṇa cites three earlier ācāryas — Auḍulomi, Āśmarathya, Kāśakṛtsna — without adjudicating their dispute. The compressed style of the *sūtra* genre forces every later reader to supply a *bhāṣya*; this is why a single text becomes the shared ground of Advaita, Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, and Bhedābheda. Whether Bādarāyaṇa's underdetermination is deliberate or merely the inheritance of an oral *sūtra*-corpus is itself an open question.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sūtra
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored as a compiled text; the work is multi-layered and likely incorporates earlier sūtra-material.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus accepts the Brahma-Sūtra as a unified compiled text traditionally ascribed to Bādarāyaṇa, with internal-style evidence of multi-layered sūtra-material.

engaged_works.summary: An aphoristic treatise: 555 *sūtras* in four *adhyāyas*, each subdivided into four *pādas*. *Adhyāya* 1 establishes *samanvaya* — that the disputed *śruti* passages all converge on *brahman* as their *tātparya*. The decisive sūtra here is 1.1.4, *tat tu samanvayāt*. *Adhyāya* 2 turns to *avirodha*: rival cosmologies are refuted in turn — Sāṅkhya *pradhāna* (2.1–2.2), Vaiśeṣika atomism, Buddhist schools (Sautrāntika, Vijñānavāda, Mādhyamika), Jaina *anekānta*, Pāśupata, Pāñcarātra. *Adhyāya* 3 treats *sādhana*: *upāsanā*, the *jīva*'s transmigration, the relation of *karma* to *vidyā*. *Adhyāya* 4 treats *phala*: *devayāna*, *kramamukti*, the condition of the *mukta-puruṣa*. The work cites by name the ācāryas Auḍulomi, Āśmarathya, Kāśakṛtsna, Bādari, Jaimini, and Kārṣṇājini; this is direct evidence that what survives is the consolidation of a prior oral *sūtra*-tradition. The text is unintelligible without a commentary, and this is precisely why it can serve as common ground for incompatible schools. Securely a compiled work; the question of single versus multi-author redaction remains open. Standard editions: Thibaut (SBE 34, 38), Deussen, and the Ānandāśrama series of the major *bhāṣyas*.
